1: <header>Overview on GHG emissions of raw milk production and a comparison of milk and cheese carbon footprints of two different systems from northern Spain </header>
Amanda Laca 1 Natalia Gómez Adriana Laca 1 Mario Díaz 1
Received: 24 May 2019 / Accepted: 22 October 2019 / Published online: 21 November 2019 Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2019
2: Milk production has been estimated to contribute 3-4% of anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. However, the carbon footprint associated with raw milk can vary, depending on a variety of factors, such as the geographical area, species of cow and production system. In this study, a global overview of research published on the carbon footprint (CF) of raw cow milk is provided. Additionally, two different dairy systems (semi-confinement and pasture-based) have been analysed by life-cycle assessment (LCA) in order to determine their effect on the CF of the milk produced. Inventory data were obtained directly from these facilities, and the main factors involved in milk production were included (co-products, livestock food, water, electricity, diesel, cleaning elements, transport, manure and slurry management, gas emissions to air etc.). In agreement with reviewed literature, it was found that the carbon footprint of milk was basically determined by the cattle feeding system and gas emissions from the cows. The values of milk CF found in the systems under study were within the range for cow milk production worldwide (0.9-4.7 kgCOeq kgfpcm Specifically, in the semi-confinement and the pasture-based dairy farms, 1.22 and 0.99 kgCOeq kgfpcm were obtained, respectively. The environmental benefits obtained with the pasture grazing system are not only mainly -1 due to the lower use of purchased fodder but also to the allocation between milk and meat that was found to be a determining methodological factor in CF calculation. Finally, data from the evaluated dairy systems have been employed to analyse the influence of raw milk production on cheese manufacturing. With this aim, the CF of a small-scale cheese factory has also been obtained. The main subsystems involved (raw materials, water, electricity, energy, cleaning products, packaging materials, transport, wastes and gas emissions) were included in the inventory of the cheese factory. CF values were 16.6 and 14.7 kgCOeq kg of cheese for milk produced in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. The production of raw milk represented more than 60% of CO2eq emissions associated with cheese, SO the primary production is the critical factor in reducing the GHG emissions due to cheese making.
Keywords LCA . Dairy farm . Raw milk production . Pasture-based . Semi-confinement Carbon footprint . Cheese

Due to growing global concern about climate change, on May 2018, the Council of the European Union formally adopted the Regulation on binding annual emission reductions by Member States from 2021 to 2030, also known as the Effort Sharing Regulation. This regulation, together with the revised ETS Directive and the LULUCF Regulation, creates a binding legal framework for the EU's efforts to reduce overall greenhouse gas emissions by at least 40% by 2030, with respect to 1990 levels (European Commission 2018). To com- municate the climate change impacts derived from food pro- duction, the carbon footprint (CF) is usually employed, a cru- cial value for indicating the GHG emissions associated with a product (Batalla et al. 2015 (Xu and Lan 2016). According to the International Dairy Federation, the calculation of the car- bon footprint of a product should be based on the LCA meth- odology included in the ISO 14000 series (IDF 2015; Abín et al. 2018; Rice et al. 2018).

The first LCA studies relating to dairy products were com- piled in the early 2000s (Finnegan et al. 2018), and the number of papers published on the environmental performance of dairy foodstuffs has increased dramatically from 2010 to date, giving a wide range of values for the CF of milk and derived products. However, Noya et al. (2018) have recently pointed out that few of these studies have focused on Spanish milk production. In addition, although it is well known that the farming system is a determining parameter for the environ- mental performance of dairy farms (Rojas-Downing et al. 2017), few studies (and none in Spain) have been carried out comparing the effect of the farming system on the milk CF (Flysjö et al. 2011; Belflower et al. 2012). The CF of the milk determines the CF of the derived dairy products, since it has been reported that raw milk production is the most significant contributor to the total impact associated with dairy products such as cheese, yogurt or processed milk (Finnegan et al. 2018; Hospido et al. 2003; Vasilaki et al. 2016). Consequently, this research has been carried out with three main objectives, firstly to carry out a mini-review on the car- bon footprint of milk worldwide, secondly, to widen knowl- edge about the carbon footprint of milk production in Spain by comparing two contrasting milk production systems and, thirdly, to analyse the effect of the milk production systems on the carbon footprint of an artisanal cheese. With this aim, two dairy farms in the same region of Spain (Asturias) have been selected as study cases, one with a typical production system of this region (a semi-confinement system) and the other with a pasture-based system. Small pasture-based farms have been recently established in this area to supply milk to local dairy facilities that demand this kind of 'greener' milk. The data for these two dairy systems have been employed to

obtain the carbon footprint of a small-scale cheese factory in the same region. The artisanal production of cheese has a strong traditional character worldwide and this cheese factory has been chosen as representative of traditional cheese pro- duction in southern Europe.

The environmental benefits obtained with the pasture grazing system are not only mainly -1 due to the lower use of purchased fodder but also to the allocation between milk and meat that was found to be a determining methodological factor in CF calculation. Finally, data from the evaluated dairy systems have been employed to analyse the influence of raw milk production on cheese manufacturing. With this aim, the CF of a small-scale cheese factory has also been obtained. The main subsystems involved (raw materials, water, electricity, energy, cleaning products, packaging materials, transport, wastes and gas emissions) were included in the inventory of the cheese factory. CF values were 16.6 and 14.7 kgCOeq kg of cheese for milk produced in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. The production of raw milk represented more than 60% of CO2eq emissions associated with cheese, SO the primary production is the critical factor in reducing the GHG emissions due to cheese making.
Keywords LCA . Dairy farm . Raw milk production . Pasture-based . Semi-confinement Carbon footprint . Cheese

seventh position in the European framework (6.8 million tonnes in 2014), with several areas of important dairy farming activities, many of them located in the Northwest of the coun- try (Noya et al. 2018).

Due to growing global concern about climate change, on May 2018, the Council of the European Union formally adopted the Regulation on binding annual emission reductions by Member States from 2021 to 2030, also known as the Effort Sharing Regulation. This regulation, together with the revised ETS Directive and the LULUCF Regulation, creates a binding legal framework for the EU's efforts to reduce overall greenhouse gas emissions by at least 40% by 2030, with respect to 1990 levels (European Commission 2018). To com- municate the climate change impacts derived from food pro- duction, the carbon footprint (CF) is usually employed, a cru- cial value for indicating the GHG emissions associated with a product (Batalla et al. 2015 (Xu and Lan 2016). According to the International Dairy Federation, the calculation of the car- bon footprint of a product should be based on the LCA meth- odology included in the ISO 14000 series (IDF 2015; Abín et al. 2018; Rice et al. 2018).

The first LCA studies relating to dairy products were com- piled in the early 2000s (Finnegan et al. 2018), and the number of papers published on the environmental performance of dairy foodstuffs has increased dramatically from 2010 to date, giving a wide range of values for the CF of milk and derived products. However, Noya et al. (2018) have recently pointed out that few of these studies have focused on Spanish milk production. In addition, although it is well known that the farming system is a determining parameter for the environ- mental performance of dairy farms (Rojas-Downing et al. 2017), few studies (and none in Spain) have been carried out comparing the effect of the farming system on the milk CF (Flysjö et al. 2011; Belflower et al. 2012). The CF of the milk determines the CF of the derived dairy products, since it has been reported that raw milk production is the most significant contributor to the total impact associated with dairy products such as cheese, yogurt or processed milk (Finnegan et al. 2018; Hospido et al. 2003; Vasilaki et al. 2016). Consequently, this research has been carried out with three main objectives, firstly to carry out a mini-review on the car- bon footprint of milk worldwide, secondly, to widen knowl- edge about the carbon footprint of milk production in Spain by comparing two contrasting milk production systems and, thirdly, to analyse the effect of the milk production systems on the carbon footprint of an artisanal cheese. With this aim, two dairy farms in the same region of Spain (Asturias) have been selected as study cases, one with a typical production system of this region (a semi-confinement system) and the other with a pasture-based system. Small pasture-based farms have been recently established in this area to supply milk to local dairy facilities that demand this kind of 'greener' milk. The data for these two dairy systems have been employed to

obtain the carbon footprint of a small-scale cheese factory in the same region. The artisanal production of cheese has a strong traditional character worldwide and this cheese factory has been chosen as representative of traditional cheese pro- duction in southern Europe.
7: Milk production has been estimated to contribute 3-4% of anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. However, the carbon footprint associated with raw milk can vary, depending on a variety of factors, such as the geographical area, species of cow and production system. In this study, a global overview of research published on the carbon footprint (CF) of raw cow milk is provided. Additionally, two different dairy systems (semi-confinement and pasture-based) have been analysed by life-cycle assessment (LCA) in order to determine their effect on the CF of the milk produced. Inventory data were obtained directly from these facilities, and the main factors involved in milk production were included (co-products, livestock food, water, electricity, diesel, cleaning elements, transport, manure and slurry management, gas emissions to air etc.). In agreement with reviewed literature, it was found that the carbon footprint of milk was basically determined by the cattle feeding system and gas emissions from the cows. The values of milk CF found in the systems under study were within the range for cow milk production worldwide (0.9-4.7 kgCOeq kgfpcm Specifically, in the semi-confinement and the pasture-based dairy farms, 1.22 and 0.99 kgCOeq kgfpcm were obtained, respectively. The environmental benefits obtained with the pasture grazing system are not only mainly -1 due to the lower use of purchased fodder but also to the allocation between milk and meat that was found to be a determining methodological factor in CF calculation. Finally, data from the evaluated dairy systems have been employed to analyse the influence of raw milk production on cheese manufacturing. With this aim, the CF of a small-scale cheese factory has also been obtained. The main subsystems involved (raw materials, water, electricity, energy, cleaning products, packaging materials, transport, wastes and gas emissions) were included in the inventory of the cheese factory. CF values were 16.6 and 14.7 kgCOeq kg of cheese for milk produced in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. The production of raw milk represented more than 60% of CO2eq emissions associated with cheese, SO the primary production is the critical factor in reducing the GHG emissions due to cheese making.
Keywords LCA . Dairy farm . Raw milk production . Pasture-based . Semi-confinement Carbon footprint . Cheese

With this aim, two dairy farms in the same region of Spain (Asturias) have been selected as study cases, one with a typical production system of this region (a semi-confinement system) and the other with a pasture-based system. Small pasture-based farms have been recently established in this area to supply milk to local dairy facilities that demand this kind of 'greener' milk. The data for these two dairy systems have been employed to

obtain the carbon footprint of a small-scale cheese factory in the same region. The artisanal production of cheese has a strong traditional character worldwide and this cheese factory has been chosen as representative of traditional cheese pro- duction in southern Europe.
8: LCA methodology was used as a tool with the aim of deter- mining the carbon footprint of the dairy farms selected as representative of two different milk production systems (a semi-confinement system and a pasture-based system). In both cases, the functional unit was defined as 1 kg of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) calculated according to the recommendations of the International Dairy Federation (IDF 2015). Additionally, the carbon footprint of a small-scale cheese factory was also calculated, employing milk from the two different dairy systems in order to determine the effect of milk origin on cheese CF. In this case, the functional unit chosen was 1 kg of cheese.
9: The analysis of the dairy farms was carried out considering a 'cradle to farm gate' perspective. The farms were in Asturias, and they are representative of milk production systems in northern Spain (an area that is responsible for the production of approximately 60% of Spanish milk). At the moment of the study, the semi-confinement farm consisted of 72 Holstein cows (48 milk producers and 24 heifers and calves) with a yearly milk production of 365,000 L. During the year of the study, 21 male calves and 7 culled cows were sold for slaugh- ter (a total of 5355 kg live weight year-1 The farm had a total of 30.45 ha of land for crops and pasture (4.61 ha for maize, 9.64 ha for grass and 16.20 for other forages). Referring now to the pasture-based model, at the moment of the study, the farm consisted of 13 heads of livestock, i.e. 11 milk-producing cows (10 Holstein and 1 Jersey) and 2 calves. During the year of the study, the production of milk was 40,730 L, 6 male calves and 3 culled COWS were sold for slaughter (a total of 2068 kg live weight year-1), whilst 2 dead calves were man- aged as dangerous wastes for incineration. The farm also had a total of 14 ha of land for crops and pasture (2 ha for maize and 12 ha for grazing).
Additionally, to analyse the effect of the milk produc- tion system on the carbon footprint of artisanal cheese, the small-scale factory described by Canellada et al. (2018) was studied.
10: The analysis of the dairy farms was carried out considering a 'cradle to farm gate' perspective. The farms were in Asturias, and they are representative of milk production systems in northern Spain (an area that is responsible for the production of approximately 60% of Spanish milk). At the moment of the study, the semi-confinement farm consisted of 72 Holstein cows (48 milk producers and 24 heifers and calves) with a yearly milk production of 365,000 L. During the year of the study, 21 male calves and 7 culled cows were sold for slaugh- ter (a total of 5355 kg live weight year-1 The farm had a total of 30.45 ha of land for crops and pasture (4.61 ha for maize, 9.64 ha for grass and 16.20 for other forages). Referring now to the pasture-based model, at the moment of the study, the farm consisted of 13 heads of livestock, i.e. 11 milk-producing cows (10 Holstein and 1 Jersey) and 2 calves. During the year of the study, the production of milk was 40,730 L, 6 male calves and 3 culled COWS were sold for slaughter (a total of 2068 kg live weight year-1), whilst 2 dead calves were man- aged as dangerous wastes for incineration. The farm also had a total of 14 ha of land for crops and pasture (2 ha for maize and 12 ha for grazing).
Additionally, to analyse the effect of the milk produc- tion system on the carbon footprint of artisanal cheese, the small-scale factory described by Canellada et al. (2018) was studied.
11: The study included the whole life cycle involved in the pro- duction of the raw milk, i.e. farming of maize, transport and production of raw materials (animal feed, cleaning products, bedding materials and drugs), consumption of water and en- ergy, cow emissions (CO2 and CH4) and management of ma- nure, slurry and wastewater. In the semi-confinement farm, cattle were fed fodder concentrate, alfalfa, hay, maize silage, meadow grass silage and, sporadically, COWS were put out to pasture. In the pasture-based farm, cows were left to graze free on grass fields during the six to seven warm months of the year, whereas during the cold months, they were housed in a stall and fed fodder concentrate, maize and hay. In the LCA inventory, only those foodstuffs that were purchased have been included, since the impact derived from the feed produc- tion in the farms had already been taken into account (land occupation and diesel consumption for farming). In the semi- confinement system, the amount of feed purchased per 1 kgfpcm was 312 g of fodder concentrate (mainly maize, soy, wheat, barley and sunflower seed), 129 g of alfalfa, 129 g of maize silage and 78 g of hay. In the pasture-based system, the amount of feed purchased per 1 kgfpcm was exclusively 206 fodder concentrate (mainly maize, soybean flour, barley, colza and beet pulp). In both systems, manure and slurry were ap- plied to the farming land. The main emission derived from this activity is ammonia, which does not contribute to the CF (Misselbrook et al. 2000). According to the Technological Institute of Renewable Energies of Spain, each bovine animal releases into the atmosphere 6.05 kg of CO2 and 0.12 kg of CH4 day (ITER 2008). These values have been used to calculate cow GHG emissions. Heifers born on the farm were employed for replacement, whereas bull calves and culled COWS were sold for meat (considered a co-product). The allo- cation factor for the dairy farms were calculated for milk as indicated by the International Dairy Federation (IDF 2015) and a value of 0.91 and 0.69 were obtained for semi- confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. These allocation factors were employed to correct the inventory data. In the case of the cheese factory, a 'cradle to retail store' perspective was considered. The principal raw material, i.e. milk, and also minority ingredients (salt and CaCl2) were in- cluded in the analysis. Packaging materials, cleaning products, tap water, electricity and transport were taken into account. Emissions from a biomass pellet boiler employed to generate heat and the production of the pellets it consumed were also included in the study. The main waste originating from the cheesemaking process is whey, which was used to feed pigs bred on a nearby farm (this was considered in the system as avoided pig fodder). The other residues were recycled or sent to landfill and wastewater was treated. In Fig. 1, an overview of the subsystems involved in this study is shown.
12: In all cases, the carbon footprint was determined by employing the Greenhouse Gas Protocol V1.01/CO2eq (kg) by means of the LCA software package SimaPro v8. This method includes scopes 1 (all direct GHG emissions), 2 (in- direct GHG emissions from consumption of purchased elec- tricity, heat or steam) and also 3 (other indirect emissions, such as transport-related activities, waste disposal, etc.) (GHGP 2017). The databases used were USLCI and EcoInvent v3.
13: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

FODDER CONCENTRATE CLEANING PRODUCTS ELECTRICITY DIESEL WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
14: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
15: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
16: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
17: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
18: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
19: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive
