legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
20: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

(2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
21: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.

With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
23: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
24: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using
FODDER CONCENTRATE ALFALFA HAY MAIZE SILAGE CLEANING PRODUCTS BEDDING MATERIAL DRUGS ELECTRICITY DIESEL TAP WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE
FODDER CONCENTRATE CLEANING PRODUCTS ELECTRICITY DIESEL WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE
Fig. 1 Overview of system boundaries considered in this work

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
25: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

FODDER CONCENTRATE CLEANING PRODUCTS ELECTRICITY DIESEL WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
26: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

FODDER CONCENTRATE CLEANING PRODUCTS ELECTRICITY DIESEL WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
27: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
28: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
29: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive
