legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
30: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
31: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
32: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

(2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
33: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
35: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using

With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
36: <header>Overview of the carbon footprint associated with raw milk production </header>

Table 1 summarises the main papers published from 2009 until the time of writing regarding the carbon footprint of cow milk production in dairy farms worldwide. The substan- tial rise in the number of studies relating to this topic published over the last year is remarkable, and indeed, almost 40% of the total were published in 2018, which points to the increasing importance of this issue (Fig. 2). Additionally, more than half of these studies have been carried out in Europe, while the rest of them have been conducted in Asia, Africa, North America and Oceania. It should be noted that there is a lack of this kind of study in South America. One recently published work analysing the CF of sheep production systems in Chile was found (Toro-Mujica et al. 2017) but none related to cow milk production. Despite the fact that the different authors employed different methodologies, in general, they agree that production of cattle feed and cow emissions are the main contributors to the milk CF. In this respect, Del Prado et al. (2013) found that lower carbon footprints of milk were gen- erally associated with cattle diets containing a lower propor- tion of food that could be used to feed humans (e.g. cereals). On the contrary, Noya et al. (2018) reported alfalfa as one of the main contributors to environmental burdens associated with feed production and proposed maize silage and grass silage as alternative ingredients. Maize silage showed potential environmental benefits in comparison with alfalfa, whereas the effect of grass silage was not SO clear, and a more detailed analysis would be needed to confirm its advantages. In this regard, Fathollahi et al. (2018) evaluated alfalfa hay and corn silage production systems as the main feedstuffs for dairy farming and concluded that alfalfa was more environmentally friendly, with lower emissions to air than corn silage. Colombini et al. (2015) compare the global warming potential (GWP) of milk production employing three forage system scenarios, based on corn, sorghum grain and sorghum forage silages and found that, although the differ- ences were very small, the lowest value was obtained using
FODDER CONCENTRATE ALFALFA HAY MAIZE SILAGE CLEANING PRODUCTS BEDDING MATERIAL DRUGS ELECTRICITY DIESEL TAP WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE
FODDER CONCENTRATE CLEANING PRODUCTS ELECTRICITY DIESEL WATER TRANSPORT LAND USE
Fig. 1 Overview of system boundaries considered in this work

a corn silage-based diet. Additionally, the cow feed does not only affect GHG emissions through its production, but the diet of the COWS also strongly influences methane emission from enteric fermentation (Colombini et al. 2015; Del Prado et al. 2013; Thomassen et al. 2009).
With respect to the production system, Flysjö et al. (2011) was not able to detect great differences in carbon footprint between a pasture grazing system in New Zealand and an

indoor housing system with the use of concentrate feed in Sweden. Soteriades et al. (2018) reported that increasing the housing period had almost no effect on the CF in pasture- based dairy farming in the UK. These same authors investi- gated the effect of replacing conventional perennial ryegrass with high-sugar grasses as a forage source and concluded that this measure allowed reductions in GWP. Fig. 2 Number of studies published from 2009 until time of writing on carbon footprint of cow milk production according to references summarised in Table 1

confinement systems in the USA. O'Brien et al. (2015), on the other hand, proposed that grass-based dairy farms would al- low higher levels of milk production per hectare, thus reduc- ing the CF and simultaneously enhancing economic performance.

Bava et al. (2014) reported that, although it was not easy to establish a clear connection between intensive farming and environmental performance, farms with COWS that are more efficient in converting feed to milk imply lower impacts per milk unit. In this context, Bakken et al. (2017) stated that an intensification of Norwegian dairy production, which implies higher yields per animal, would contribute to scoring more favourably in terms of GWP. Woldegebriel et al. (2017), who studied milk production in Ethiopia at three different scales, found that in small traditional farms the environmental impacts per cattle unit were lower than in large-scale farms. However, the milk yield per cow was higher in the large-scale farms, SO that the impacts on per kilogramme of milk did not differ significantly between the three scales analysed. These authors also concluded that the impacts on the global warming potential category seen in the Ethiopian dairy farms were higher than those reported for the same type of farms in de- veloped countries due to the low-quality forages and wheat bran used to feed the animals.

Some authors also identified manure management prac- tices as one of the critical factors for milk production. Castanheira et al. (2010) pointed out that more than 70% of the CO2eq generated from milk production was due to CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The manure management choice was one of the factors re- sponsible for differences found between five milk production regions in the USA (Thoma et al. 2013). Additionally, manure management practices were also identified as an important factor in reducing the carbon footprint in Australia (Gollnow et al. 2014), and Fan et al. (2018) reported that, in China, the fully coupled mode, which recycles both solid and liquid
animal manure, implies lower GHG emissions than the semi-coupled mode, which only recycles solid manure to farmlands.

An important methodological issue, which is in many cases crucial for the outcome of a raw milk CF, is the consideration of the coproduction of other products on the farm, such as the sale of cattle for slaughter. In fact, most studies recently pub- lished on milk CF include allocation between milk and meat (Gollnow et al. 2014; IDF 2015).

The geographical area is a parameter that also affects the environmental performance of a farm. This factor determines the climate, and therefore the possibilities for animal feeding, and also the degree of technological and legislative development. Weiss and Leip (2012) analysed the GHG fluxes from the livestock sector for all EU-27 countries and concluded that the countries with the lowest net product emis- sions did not necessarily use similar production systems. Figure 3 shows the values for carbon footprint of raw cow milk VS. Human Development Index (HDI) (UNDP 2018) in the countries where the study was carried out. Data from Table 1 have been standardised to 1 kgfpcm, and CF have been recalculated according to equations reported by Lorenz et al. (2019), considering the average composition of cow milk (FAO 2019). Only real data has been employed for the graph, i.e. data obtained from model farms have not been included, and, when reviewed works reported a range of CF values, maximum and minimum values have been represented. Data are shown in three groups, depending on the allocation meth- od employed in the cited work, since it has been reported that the choice of allocation method can substantially affect the CF value (Lorenz et al. 2019). A priori, it would be expected that, in general, CF values associated with milk production should be higher in less developed countries, due to a variety of factors, such as the lower technological development, the em- ployment of low-quality forages to feed the dairy cows, the lower yield per animal and also the more permissive

When|3.3| reviewed works reported a range|| of CF values, maximum and|3.0| minimum values have been rep-|2.7| resented. The FU has been|2.4| standardised to 1 kgfpcm and CF|2.1| has been recalculated (Lorenz et al. 2019). legislation on environmental issues. However, it is noteworthy that no correlation between CF and HDI could be observed for any of the three groups of data analysed. Differences between the groups with different allocation methods could not be identified either, although it is obvious that for the same sys- tem different allocation methods give different results. Additionally, another aspect that should be noticed is the high variability of CF values found for the same country, even when the same allocation method is employed. See for in- stance, Kenya or Spain, which showed two extreme values for carbon footprint (0.9 and 4.3 and 0.8 and 2.1 kgCOeq kgfpcm respectively). These great differences can be attrib- uted to a variety of factors, such as diet/feeding system, cli- mate, management practices and also, but not only, the allo- cation method selected when other products are coproduced.
37: <header>Comparison of carbon footprint of milk production in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems </header>

In order to illustrate the effect of the system employed on the farm on the milk CF, data from two real farms were employed to calculate CF values. Both farms were located in the same geographical region, and, in both cases, calves were sold as a co-product and allocation was considered.

As can be seen in Fig. 4, results obtained with the Greenhouse Gas Protocol for the two farms revealed cow feeding and cow emissions to air as the factors with the highest environmental loads in carbon footprint values, which agrees with results found in the literature and mentioned above. Specifically, food was responsible for more than 40 and 20% of the CF in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. This difference is due to the fact that in pasture-based systems cows were fed mainly by being left free to graze on grasslands, SO only 206 g of food not culti- vated in situ was needed to produce 1 kgfpcm, whereas in the semi-confinement system 648 g of ex situ food were

necessary. It is well known that cow emissions (mainly orig- inated by enteric fermentation) are associated with extensive cattle production (De Oliveira and Bourscheidt 2017; Coates et al. 2017). In this study, to produce 1 kgfpcm, 419 and 505 g of GHG were emitted by the COWS in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively, and these quantities cor- respond to 52 and 78% of the CF.

According to the ISO 14067 standard, both biogenic and fossil carbon should be included in carbon footprint calcula- tions. Following this guideline, values of 1.25 and 1.03 kgCOeq kgfpcm were obtained in the semi-confinement and the pasture-based dairy farms, respectively. As explained in the 'Materials and methods' section, the emissions of GHG from the cow metabolism were calculated using theoretical data (ITER 2008). It is well known that these emissions de- pend on different factors, such as cow diet and breed. Nevertheless, if it were assumed that cow GHG emissions were 10% lower, the reduction in milk CF would be lower than 8% in both the analysed systems. The CF values obtained in this research are within the range found in literature for cow milk production worldwide (0.9-4.7 7kgCO2eq kgfpcm and considerably lower than values reported for other dairy ani- mals (sheep, goat and buffalo) (1.9-5.2 kg CO2eq kg milk) (Weiss and Leip 2012; Batalla et al. 2015; Patra 2017).

Thus, according to the results obtained in the present study, and considering the carbon footprint as indicator of the envi- ronmental impact, the pasture-based dairy farm was shown to be a greener production system than the semi-confinement farm. These data are in agreement to those reported by O'Brien et al. (2015) who indicated that an increase in the length of the grazing season implies a reduction in the CF. In the case of the semi-confinement farm considered here, if the amount of pasture intake were increased, SO that the amount of purchased food could be reduced by just 10%, the value of the milk CF would be 1.20, instead of 1.25 kgCOeq kgfpcm Belflower et al. (2012) and Flysjö et al.
Fig. 4 Carbon footprint of raw milk obtained from Green House Gas Protocol: a semi-confinement dairy farm and b pasture-based dairy farm (FU = 1 kgfpcm of raw milk)

(2011) also obtained lower values for pasture-based systems than for confinement systems, although the differences found were relatively small. In recent work on this question, Lorenz et al. (2019) carried out a methodological approach to com- pare milk production systems with different management op- tions and concluded that, at a constant milk yield, the CF was significantly lower in the pasture systems compared with the semi-confinement and confinement systems.

In terms of productivity, each cow produced 7308 kgfpcm year in the semi-confinement system, a value very similar to that reported by Wang et al. (2018) for the North China Plain in 2015 (7000 kgfpcm cow-1, whereas in the pasture-based system, each cow produced only 3618 kgfpcm year It is well known that productivity of milking COWS shows a strong negative correlation with the CF of milk. However, in the case analysed here, the benefits derived from using a pasture-based

system balanced the disadvantages derived from the lower productivity. Additionally, there was a great difference be- tween the allocation factors for the dairy farms under study (0.91 and 0.69 for semi-confinement and pasture-based sys- items, respectively). This means that the amount of meat pro- duced on the farm from the sale of culled COWS and surplus calves is determining for the value of the milk carbon footprint and in the present case makes the scale tip in favour of the pasture-based system. Certainly, if the amount of meat pro- duced per milking cow in the pasture-based system were the same as in the semi-confinement system, the allocation factor would change from 0.69 to 0.81 and the CF would be 1.21 kgCOeq kgfpcm a value much closer to the CF obtained for the semi-confinement system.
Finally, it should be remarked that, as can be seen in Fig. 4, fossil carbon contributed almost 50% of the total CF in the

semi-confinement daily farm, whereas it represented approxi- mately 25% of the CF of milk from the pasture-based farm. Biogenic carbon comes from biological processes, and it is part of the fast domain of the global carbon cycle. On the contrary, emissions from fossil sources contribute to the atmospheric pool by releasing carbon from the geologic pool and are there- fore new emissions to the atmosphere (Breton et al. 2018). In this respect, Gunn et al. (2012) affirmed that biogenic emissions are less harmful than fossil emissions. Thus, regarding GHG emission, this is also an advantage of the pasture-based system compared with the semi-confinement system.

This means that the amount of meat pro- duced on the farm from the sale of culled COWS and surplus calves is determining for the value of the milk carbon footprint and in the present case makes the scale tip in favour of the pasture-based system. Certainly, if the amount of meat pro- duced per milking cow in the pasture-based system were the same as in the semi-confinement system, the allocation factor would change from 0.69 to 0.81 and the CF would be 1.21 kgCOeq kgfpcm a value much closer to the CF obtained for the semi-confinement system.
Finally, it should be remarked that, as can be seen in Fig. 4, fossil carbon contributed almost 50% of the total CF in the

semi-confinement daily farm, whereas it represented approxi- mately 25% of the CF of milk from the pasture-based farm. Biogenic carbon comes from biological processes, and it is part of the fast domain of the global carbon cycle. On the contrary, emissions from fossil sources contribute to the atmospheric pool by releasing carbon from the geologic pool and are there- fore new emissions to the atmosphere (Breton et al. 2018). In this respect, Gunn et al. (2012) affirmed that biogenic emissions are less harmful than fossil emissions. Thus, regarding GHG emission, this is also an advantage of the pasture-based system compared with the semi-confinement system.
39: <header>Comparison of carbon footprint of milk production in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems </header>

In order to illustrate the effect of the system employed on the farm on the milk CF, data from two real farms were employed to calculate CF values. Both farms were located in the same geographical region, and, in both cases, calves were sold as a co-product and allocation was considered.

As can be seen in Fig. 4, results obtained with the Greenhouse Gas Protocol for the two farms revealed cow feeding and cow emissions to air as the factors with the highest environmental loads in carbon footprint values, which agrees with results found in the literature and mentioned above. Specifically, food was responsible for more than 40 and 20% of the CF in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. This difference is due to the fact that in pasture-based systems cows were fed mainly by being left free to graze on grasslands, SO only 206 g of food not culti- vated in situ was needed to produce 1 kgfpcm, whereas in the semi-confinement system 648 g of ex situ food were

necessary. It is well known that cow emissions (mainly orig- inated by enteric fermentation) are associated with extensive cattle production (De Oliveira and Bourscheidt 2017; Coates et al. 2017). In this study, to produce 1 kgfpcm, 419 and 505 g of GHG were emitted by the COWS in semi-confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively, and these quantities cor- respond to 52 and 78% of the CF.

According to the ISO 14067 standard, both biogenic and fossil carbon should be included in carbon footprint calcula- tions. Following this guideline, values of 1.25 and 1.03 kgCOeq kgfpcm were obtained in the semi-confinement and the pasture-based dairy farms, respectively. As explained in the 'Materials and methods' section, the emissions of GHG from the cow metabolism were calculated using theoretical data (ITER 2008). It is well known that these emissions de- pend on different factors, such as cow diet and breed. Nevertheless, if it were assumed that cow GHG emissions were 10% lower, the reduction in milk CF would be lower than 8% in both the analysed systems. The CF values obtained in this research are within the range found in literature for cow milk production worldwide (0.9-4.7 7kgCO2eq kgfpcm and considerably lower than values reported for other dairy ani- mals (sheep, goat and buffalo) (1.9-5.2 kg CO2eq kg milk) (Weiss and Leip 2012; Batalla et al. 2015; Patra 2017).

Thus, according to the results obtained in the present study, and considering the carbon footprint as indicator of the envi- ronmental impact, the pasture-based dairy farm was shown to be a greener production system than the semi-confinement farm. These data are in agreement to those reported by O'Brien et al. (2015) who indicated that an increase in the length of the grazing season implies a reduction in the CF. In the case of the semi-confinement farm considered here, if the amount of pasture intake were increased, SO that the amount of purchased food could be reduced by just 10%, the value of the milk CF would be 1.20, instead of 1.25 kgCOeq kgfpcm Belflower et al. (2012) and Flysjö et al.
Fig. 4 Carbon footprint of raw milk obtained from Green House Gas Protocol: a semi-confinement dairy farm and b pasture-based dairy farm (FU = 1 kgfpcm of raw milk)

(2011) also obtained lower values for pasture-based systems than for confinement systems, although the differences found were relatively small. In recent work on this question, Lorenz et al. (2019) carried out a methodological approach to com- pare milk production systems with different management op- tions and concluded that, at a constant milk yield, the CF was significantly lower in the pasture systems compared with the semi-confinement and confinement systems.

In terms of productivity, each cow produced 7308 kgfpcm year in the semi-confinement system, a value very similar to that reported by Wang et al. (2018) for the North China Plain in 2015 (7000 kgfpcm cow-1, whereas in the pasture-based system, each cow produced only 3618 kgfpcm year It is well known that productivity of milking COWS shows a strong negative correlation with the CF of milk. Thus, regarding GHG emission, this is also an advantage of the pasture-based system compared with the semi-confinement system.

44: <header>Effect of milk production system on carbon footprint of artisanal cheese factory </header>
Figure 5 shows the carbon footprint values obtained with the Greenhouse Gas Protocol in an artisanal cheese factory employing milk from a semi-confinement dairy farm and milk

from a pasture-based dairy farm. In both cases, the production of raw milk contributed more than 60% of COeq emissions (62 and 67% from semi-confinement and pasture-based sys- items, respectively). Considering the fossil and the biogenic carbon, the carbon footprint values were 16.9 and 15.0 kgCO2eq kg of cheese for milk produced in semi- confinement and pasture-based systems, respectively. So, it is noticeable that the employment of raw milk from a pasture-based system instead of from a semi-confinement based system was able to reduce the carbon footprint of cheese by more than 10%. Santos Jr. et al. (2017) found that global warming potential emissions of cheese production in a small- sized dairy industry in Brazil were 14.4 kg CO2eq kg-1 of product, which is a very similar value to that obtained here, employing milk from pasture-based systems (14.7 kgCO2eq kg- of cheese).
Thus, reducing the impact of milk production is the key parameter for decreasing the carbon footprint of
Fig. 5 Carbon footprint of cheese obtained from Green House Gas Protocol: a employing milk from semi-confinement dairy farm and b employing milk from pasture- based dairy farm (FU = 1 kg of cheese)

cheese manufacture and various factors are involved in this question not only cow feeding (as it was mentioned above) but also intensification, manure management practices and even the cow breed are also important aspects to be considered (Capper and Cady 2012; Kristensen et al. 2015).

46: The number of papers published on the CF of raw milk has dramatically increased in the last year, it being noticeable that few of these works were carried out in Spain. CF values showed high variability worldwide and no correlation with the degree of development of the country was found. This can be attributed to different factors, not determined by the geographical area, such as management practices, degree of intensification, multi-functionality, cow breed and/or co- production of meat.

Results obtained in the two cases studies analysed in this work indicated that, in agreement with the literature, the production of the food and the cow gas emissions to air were the most important contributors to the milk CF. Milk produced in the pasture-based system showed a CF 18% lower than in the case of the semi-confinement sys- tem. So, increasing the grazing season seems a good way to reduce the GHG emissions. Additionally, the number of animals sold for meat was also found to be a determining factor. Biogenic carbon represented almost half of milk CF in the semi-confinement system and around 75% of the CF of milk from the pasture-based system. Since fos- sil emissions are usually considered more harmful than biogenic emissions, this is also an advantage of pasture- based farms. When milk from the pasture-based system was employed as the raw material to produce cheese, the cheese CF was 11% lower than the value obtained when milk from semi-confinement system was used.

To sum up, the main improvement actions to reduce the CF of raw milk would be the modification of feeding practices to minimise the use of fodder concentrates produced off-farm and at the same time increasing the co-production of by-prod- ucts, such as meat, in order to share the burdens associated with the farm performance.
